\newcommand{\fullkv}{FullKV}
\newcommand{\bench}{PriorityBench-A}

\title{PriorityKV: A Measurement Study of Structure-Aware KV Retention\\
  and Mixed-Precision Storage for Long Agent Traces}
\author[1]{Arush Sharma}
\author[2]{Anupam Rawart}
\affil[1]{Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines) Dhanbad}
\affil[2]{Indian Institute of Technology Bombay}
\date{}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
Long language-model agent traces contain heterogeneous state---tool schemas,
system instructions, superseding constraints, identifiers, dialogue, and
filler---whose tokens occupy the key--value (KV) cache with very different
behavioral consequences when lost. We study whether cache allocation should
use this application-visible trace structure, and we report the results as a
scoped measurement study on Qwen3-8B (H200). We introduce \bench{}, a locked
240-example benchmark with deterministic scorers covering tool-schema
conformance, instruction supersession, and multi-turn state at 8k, 16k, and
32k context strata. Under matched-budget eviction stress at a 25\% keep
budget, a role-blind sink-and-recent policy scores $0.000$ while
structure-aware retention scores $1.000$ at token granularity and $0.643$ at
16-token page granularity ($n{=}14$ per slice); an adversarial buried-state
variant lowers the structure score to $0.429$, marking the boundary of the
heuristic. Separately, we falsify the hypothesis that soft INT4 storage for
75\% of positions creates a meaningful quality separation: on all 240 locked
examples, \fullkv{}, role-blind mixed, and structure-aware mixed score
$0.8875$, $0.8792$, and $0.8833$. We therefore evaluate the mixed
BF16/INT4 path as a systems mechanism only. Our packed cache with
FlashInfer-merged decode uses $0.72\times$ measured KV payload bytes and
$0.87\times$ peak allocated CUDA memory relative to \fullkv{}, while
end-to-end latency is $1.11$--$1.12\times$ and time per output token is
$1.20$--$1.21\times$: the current implementation is a bytes win and a latency
cost, and the gap between payload and peak is caused by an explicit
INT4-to-BF16 cold-attention scratch buffer. The study supports three scoped
conclusions: agent-trace structure is a useful retention prior under
aggressive eviction, soft INT4 is quality-neutral at the tested operating
point, and packed bytes do not automatically imply peak-memory or latency
wins.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}\label{sec:intro}

Autoregressive Transformer inference stores the keys and values of previous
tokens so that generation does not recompute the complete prefix
\cite{vaswani2017attention}. The resulting KV cache grows with context length
and batch size and is a central memory-management concern in LLM serving
\cite{kwon2023pagedattention}. Existing work reduces this cost through
paging, eviction, token selection, or quantization
\cite{kwon2023pagedattention,xiao2024streamingllm,liu2023scissorhands,zhang2023h2o,li2024snapkv,cai2024pyramidkv,liu2024kivi,ye2025flashinfer}.
These methods generally estimate token importance from position, attention,
or activation statistics.

Agent traces expose another signal before any attention is evaluated: their
protocol structure. A serving layer often knows which spans are system
instructions, tool schemas, tool results, user constraints, or generated
dialogue. A role-blind cache policy can therefore satisfy a nominal token
budget while deleting state that is necessary for the agent's next action,
and aggregate language-model metrics need not expose the resulting failures:
a malformed tool call, compliance with an obsolete instruction, or use of the
wrong order identifier.

PriorityKV asks two questions that must remain separate, because eviction
removes information while quantization retains an approximation of it:

\begin{enumerate}
\item At a matched eviction budget, does preserving protocol structure
  improve agent-task reliability over role-blind position policies?
\item If low-precision KV storage is quality-neutral, can a working mixed
  BF16/INT4 cache still provide measurable systems value?
\end{enumerate}

Our initial expectation was that role-aware INT4 placement would create a
quality gap. It did not at the selected 75\% INT4 operating point
(Section~\ref{sec:results-int4}). We retain that negative result and evaluate
the packed path in bytes and latency instead.

\paragraph{Contributions.} This report makes four contributions:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{\bench{}:} a reproducible 240-example agent-reliability
  benchmark with deterministic scorers and a frozen manifest hash
  (Section~\ref{sec:bench}).
\item \textbf{Matched-budget eviction evidence:} structure-aware retention
  outperforms role-blind sink-and-recent, random, and fixed-prefix controls
  on targeted stress slices, with adversarial buried-state and
  middle-relocation checks that scope the result
  (Section~\ref{sec:results-eviction}).
\item \textbf{A falsified quality hypothesis:} role-blind and
  structure-aware mixed INT4 policies both remain close to \fullkv{} on the
  locked 240-example evaluation (Section~\ref{sec:results-int4}).
\item \textbf{An auditable systems prototype:} packed BF16/INT4 pages,
  FlashInfer log-sum-exp state merging, phase-separated latency, and separate
  reporting of payload and peak memory on H200
  (Sections~\ref{sec:method-decode} and~\ref{sec:results-systems}).
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Scope of claims.} The report deliberately does \emph{not} claim:
paper-grade generalization across LongBench \cite{bai2024longbench} or RULER
\cite{hsieh2024ruler}; a quantization-driven accuracy advantage; a latency or
serving-throughput win from the current implementation; a peak-memory
collapse (the cold-scratch buffer prevents it); or that the reduced Gemma
check reproduces the Qwen results in absolute terms. The decisive eviction
slices contain 14--16 examples each and are reported as controlled stress
tests, not as a broad benchmark matrix.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig_overview.pdf}
  \caption{Conceptual overview of PriorityKV (illustration, not measured
  data). An agent trace arrives with known message roles (top strip). A
  structural tagger maps spans to page roles and protects a 16-token sink and
  a 128-token recent window; a matched allocator then applies the same keep
  or INT4 budget as every baseline arm, differing only in \emph{which} pages
  it protects; storage is a paged KV cache with BF16 hot pages and packed
  INT4 cold pages. Bottom left: role-blind sink-and-recent eviction can
  delete agent-critical middle state. Bottom right: the mixed-precision cache
  retains every position and lowers precision on filler instead. The tagger
  uses prompt metadata only---never future attention or benchmark answers.}
  \label{fig:overview}
\end{figure}

Figure~\ref{fig:overview} summarizes the system. The diagram follows the
token-strip and left-to-right cache-workflow conventions common in H2O
\cite{zhang2023h2o}, SnapKV \cite{li2024snapkv}, and KIVI \cite{liu2024kivi},
but depicts PriorityKV's distinct input signal and allocation policy: unlike
attention-derived selection, PriorityKV begins from message-role metadata
already present in the trace.

\section{Related Work}\label{sec:related}

\paragraph{KV memory management.} PagedAttention reduces fragmentation and
enables flexible KV sharing without changing which tokens are retained
\cite{kwon2023pagedattention}. StreamingLLM observes the role of attention
sinks and combines initial tokens with a recent window
\cite{xiao2024streamingllm}. We use this sink-and-recent shape as a
deliberately role-blind eviction control, not as a complete reimplementation
or evaluation of StreamingLLM.

\paragraph{KV eviction and token selection.} Scissorhands retains tokens that
were previously important \cite{liu2023scissorhands}, while H2O balances
heavy hitters with recent tokens \cite{zhang2023h2o}. SnapKV estimates prompt
importance from an observation window \cite{li2024snapkv}, and PyramidKV
varies the budget across layers \cite{cai2024pyramidkv}. These methods derive
importance from model behavior. PriorityKV instead asks whether
application-visible message roles are useful when the workload is an agent
trace. The approaches are complementary---structural priors could be combined
with attention-based selection---but that combination is not evaluated here,
and Section~\ref{sec:limitations} lists the absence of tuned H2O/SnapKV
baselines as a limitation.

\paragraph{KV quantization.} KIVI motivates asymmetric low-bit treatment of
keys and values and demonstrates that aggressive KV quantization can preserve
downstream quality \cite{liu2024kivi}. Our INT4 path is not proposed as a
replacement for KIVI or as a new quantizer. It is a storage and measurement
vehicle for comparing role-aware and role-blind precision allocation under a
matched budget.

\paragraph{Serving and evaluation.} FlashInfer provides composable attention
kernels and state merging for heterogeneous serving workloads
\cite{ye2025flashinfer}. PriorityKV uses its \texttt{merge\_state} primitive
to combine hot BF16 and dequantized cold attention results
(Section~\ref{sec:method-decode}). LongBench \cite{bai2024longbench} and
RULER \cite{hsieh2024ruler} provide broad long-context evaluations. \bench{}
is narrower: it isolates deterministic agent-state failures and should be
read as a complementary diagnostic rather than a general long-context
benchmark.

\section{PriorityBench-A}\label{sec:bench}

\subsection{Tasks and construction}\label{sec:bench-tasks}

\bench{} contains 240 generated multi-turn conversations, with 80 examples in
each of three categories (Table~\ref{tab:tasks}). Each conversation plants
relevant state early, adds benign filler, and ends with a request whose
correct answer depends on that state. Scoring is programmatic (0/1 per
example) and does not use an LLM-as-judge.

\begin{table}[t]
  \centering
  \caption{The three \bench{} task categories (80 examples each). Every
  scorer is deterministic; a slice score is the mean of per-example 0/1
  outcomes.}
  \label{tab:tasks}
  \small
  \begin{tabular}{@{}lll@{}}
    \toprule
    Category & Required behavior & Scoring \\
    \midrule
    Tool schema & Emit a valid call under an earlier tool contract &
      JSON parsing plus schema-subset checks \\
    Instruction supersession & Follow the latest of conflicting constraints &
      Required and forbidden regular expressions \\
    Multi-turn state & Reuse an earlier ID, path, or preference &
      Exact slot match \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

The lock contains 83 examples at 8k, 81 at 16k, and 76 at 32k tokens; split
counts are 92 calibration, 49 validation, and 99 test, assigned by stable
hashing. The frozen manifest has SHA256
\begin{center}
\texttt{fc44b966725738c94008ba61ce57ad7366169b9c0be73074f8161d909ccfae89}.
\end{center}

\bench{} is synthetic by design. It offers controlled failure attribution and
exact scoring, but it does not estimate the frequency of these failures in
production traffic. The generator, templates, manifest, and scorers are
included in the repository.

\subsection{Adversarial variants and leakage controls}\label{sec:bench-adv}

The initial templates often place short, gold-bearing turns before long
filler, so a policy that simply prefers short or early spans could appear
semantic without being so. Two controls address this:

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Buried state:} gold-bearing turns are lengthened or buried in
  filler so that short-turn tagging no longer identifies all state (40 of the
  240 locked examples carry a buried-state flag).
\item \textbf{Middle relocation:} the state block is moved away from the
  prefix while preserving the leading system message and final request,
  separating structural retention from mere early-position retention.
\end{itemize}

These variants exposed a real limitation: the structure policy preserves tool
and constraint roles more reliably than unmarked free-form state
(Section~\ref{sec:results-eviction}). An early tagger implementation keyed on
final-answer markup was removed; the buried-state and middle-relocation
controls were added specifically to detect such leakage.

\section{Method}\label{sec:method}

\subsection{Structural roles}\label{sec:method-roles}

PriorityKV maps chat messages to page roles using information already
available to an agent serving stack. System messages are tagged as system,
tool, or constraint spans; tool messages and schema-like assistant messages
are tagged as tool spans; constraint-like user messages are tagged as
constraints; and short state-bearing turns can be tagged as other structured
state. Independent of roles, the first 16 tokens are protected as an
attention sink and the most recent 128 tokens are protected as a decode-local
window.

The tagger is a transparent heuristic over message roles, message length, and
a small set of schema and constraint markers. It is not a learned importance
model and does not inspect the benchmark answer.

\subsection{Matched-budget eviction}\label{sec:method-eviction}

For a prompt of $n$ tokens and keep fraction $k \in (0,1]$, define the
nominal retention budget
\begin{equation}
  B(n,k) = \operatorname{round}(k\,n).
\end{equation}
Let $S$ be the sink positions (first 16 tokens), $R$ the recent window (last
128 tokens), and $M = S \cup R$ the mandatory set shared by every arm. Each
arm selects a retained set $A \subseteq \{1,\dots,n\}$ with
\begin{equation}
  M \subseteq A, \qquad |A| = \max\bigl(B(n,k),\, |M|\bigr),
\end{equation}
so all arms receive exactly the same budget and differ only in how they fill
$A \setminus M$:

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Role-blind sink+recent:} fill the budget backward from the
  recent edge (the StreamingLLM-shaped control).
\item \textbf{Random:} sample the remaining positions or pages uniformly.
\item \textbf{Fixed prefix:} take the lowest-index pages, a control that
  distinguishes structural retention from early-position retention.
\item \textbf{Structure-aware:} retain protected-role pages first, then fill
  any remaining budget near the recent edge.
\item \textbf{Keep-all:} retain the complete prompt, as a wiring control.
\end{itemize}

Page-granularity experiments operate on contiguous pages of $P{=}16$ tokens.
Token-granularity selection gathers the retained positions and regenerates
the cache so that positions are re-indexed contiguously, avoiding an invalid
RoPE index substitution. These eviction experiments isolate missing-state
sensitivity; they are not the packed mixed-precision serving path of
Section~\ref{sec:method-mixed}.

The role-blind sink-and-recent arm is expected to fail whenever required
state sits in the evicted middle; the informative comparisons are therefore
structure against the random and fixed-prefix controls at the same budget,
and the adversarial variants that probe what the heuristic misses.

\subsection{Matched mixed precision}\label{sec:method-mixed}

The mixed-precision policy assigns the same number of INT4 positions in both
arms. With target fraction $f = 0.75$ of the cache, sink and recent positions
always remain BF16, and both arms demote exactly the same count $N_4$ of
positions (the realized fraction in the frozen run is $0.750$). The
role-blind arm spaces INT4 positions uniformly through the demotable region;
the structure arm demotes filler and low-risk roles first, spilling into
protected roles only when required to meet the exact budget.

For the frozen Qwen3-8B path, K and V are divided into 16-token pages. Hot
pages store BF16 tensors; cold pages store packed 4-bit values (two per byte)
plus per-group scale metadata. Packing is real storage, not a fake-quantized
BF16 tensor. Byte accounting reports both the actual payload (packed values
plus metadata) and an idealized 0.5-byte-per-element model, and the two are
deliberately kept distinct in Section~\ref{sec:results-systems}.

\subsection{FlashInfer decode with LSE merging}\label{sec:method-decode}

Prefill uses the native Hugging Face path. The cache is then split into hot
BF16 pages and cold packed pages. During decode, the Qwen3 attention shim
performs at most two attention calls per layer: one over the coalesced hot
pages and the decode tail, and one over cold pages after dequantization. Each
call returns a partial attention output with its log-sum-exp (LSE) state;
FlashInfer's \texttt{merge\_state} combines the two partial softmax states
into the exact attention result over the union of pages. Parity tests against
a monolithic reference reduced the maximum absolute merge error to
approximately $4.88 \times 10^{-4}$ after correcting an LSE-base mismatch.

The current implementation expands cold INT4 pages into a BF16 GPU scratch
buffer before the cold attention call (Figure~\ref{fig:dataflow}). This
avoids silently materializing a full Hugging Face cache, but it limits peak
memory savings and adds decode overhead; it is the single mechanism behind
both the payload-versus-peak gap and the TPOT regression in
Section~\ref{sec:results-systems}. Direct fused or page-streamed INT4
attention is future systems work.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig_decode_dataflow.pdf}
  \caption{Conceptual dataflow of the mixed-precision decode path
  (illustration, not measured data). Hot BF16 pages and the decode tail feed
  one FlashInfer attention call; cold packed INT4 pages are first dequantized
  into a BF16 scratch buffer and feed a second call; \texttt{merge\_state}
  combines the two log-sum-exp states into the exact softmax over all pages
  (maximum absolute parity error ${\approx}4.88 \times 10^{-4}$). The scratch
  buffer is drawn explicitly because it is the mechanism that limits peak
  memory to $0.87\times$ \fullkv{} despite a $0.72\times$ payload, and it
  contributes to the ${\sim}1.20\times$ TPOT cost
  (Section~\ref{sec:results-systems}).}
  \label{fig:dataflow}
\end{figure}

\section{Experimental Setup}\label{sec:setup}

The primary experiments use Qwen3-8B \cite{yang2025qwen3} in non-thinking
greedy decode mode on NVIDIA H200 GPUs, at the pinned model revision recorded
in \texttt{FINAL\_RUN\_MANIFEST.yaml} together with the benchmark hash,
frozen configurations, job IDs, and pass/fail decisions. The evaluation
families answer different questions and have deliberately different sizes
(Table~\ref{tab:families}).

\begin{table}[t]
  \centering
  \caption{Evaluation families. The decisive eviction slices are small by
  design (controlled stress tests); the 240-example lock measures mixed
  INT4 quality, not eviction.}
  \label{tab:families}
  \small
  \begin{tabular}{@{}lrl@{}}
    \toprule
    Family & Size & Purpose \\
    \midrule
    Matched-keep stress & 14--16 per configuration &
      Controlled eviction sensitivity and policy ablations \\
    Lock-240 mixed quality & 240 &
      Quality of \fullkv{} and matched 75\% INT4 placement \\
    D4 latency & 18 (9 per 8k/16k stratum) &
      Pack, cold-scratch, TTFT, and TPOT measurement \\
    Peak and payload & 18 &
      Allocated/reserved CUDA peak and cache payload bytes \\
    Gemma reduced & 14 at ${\approx}$8k &
      Directional second-model check only \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

Latency rows use 128 generated tokens, greedy decoding, one warm-up sequence,
and three timing repeats whose median is recorded. \fullkv{} uses the SDPA
attention path; mixed arms use the FlashInfer shim, so the latency comparison
includes the backend difference and is not kernel-isolated. This is a
single-request prototype comparison, not a throughput or concurrency study.
An FP8 baseline job uses a different batch-amortized timing protocol and is
therefore retained as a pass/fail secondary check rather than merged into the
main latency table.

\section{Results}\label{sec:results}

\subsection{Structure matters under matched-budget eviction}
\label{sec:results-eviction}

Table~\ref{tab:eviction} reports the matched-budget eviction slices and
Figure~\ref{fig:sweep} plots the page-budget sweep. At a 25\% token keep
budget, the role-blind sink-and-recent arm scores $0.000$ while
structure-aware retention scores $1.000$ (14/14). At page granularity the
structure arm reaches $0.643$ (9/14) and stays on that plateau across the
15--35\% budget sweep, while the random control climbs from $0.071$ to
$0.429$ as the budget grows.

\begin{table}[t]
  \centering
  \caption{Matched-budget eviction stress on Qwen3-8B. All arms in a row
  receive the same budget; ``---'' means the control was not run in that
  slice. Scores are means of deterministic 0/1 outcomes; at these sample
  sizes one example changes a score by ${\sim}0.06$--$0.07$. The same
  14-example slice is reused across the page-budget sweep, so those rows are
  an ablation over one fixed slice, not independent replications.}
  \label{tab:eviction}
  \small
  \begin{tabular}{@{}lrrrrrr@{}}
    \toprule
    Setting & $n$ & Keep-all & Role-blind & Random & Fixed prefix &
      Structure \\
    \midrule
    Token keep, $k{=}0.25$          & 14 & 1.000 & 0.000 & 0.000 & --- &
      \textbf{1.000} \\
    Page keep, $k{=}0.15$           & 14 & 1.000 & 0.000 & 0.071 & --- &
      \textbf{0.643} \\
    Page keep, $k{=}0.25$           & 14 & 1.000 & 0.000 & 0.286 & --- &
      \textbf{0.643} \\
    Page keep, $k{=}0.35$           & 14 & 1.000 & 0.000 & 0.429 & --- &
      \textbf{0.643} \\
    Buried token state, $k{=}0.25$  & 14 & 1.000 & 0.000 & 0.000 & --- &
      \textbf{0.429} \\
    Middle-relocated pages, $k{=}0.25$ & 16 & 1.000 & 0.000 & --- & 0.125 &
      \textbf{0.688} \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

Three observations follow. First, the role-blind zero is expected---a
sink-and-recent window necessarily deletes required middle state at these
budgets---and it quantifies how brittle that default shape is on agent
traces. Second, the structure arm's $0.643$ plateau and its drop to $0.429$
(6/14) under buried state are equally important: explicit roles rescue a
subset of failures, but the heuristic does not identify all free-form
multi-turn state. Third, middle relocation separates structure ($0.688$,
11/16) from the fixed-prefix control ($0.125$, 2/16), showing that the effect
is not merely early-position retention.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig_keep_budget_curve.pdf}
  \caption{Measured score versus matched keep budget $k$ for
  page-granularity eviction (Qwen3-8B, 16-token pages, same fixed
  14-example stress slice at every budget). Role-blind sink-and-recent stays
  at $0.000$ across budgets; random retention climbs with budget
  ($0.071 \to 0.429$); structure-aware retention holds a $0.643$ plateau;
  keep-all confirms $1.000$ wiring. Whiskers are Wilson 95\% intervals for
  $n{=}14$ and points are dodged horizontally for legibility. The sweep is
  an ablation over one slice, not three independent replications
  (Table~\ref{tab:eviction}).}
  \label{fig:sweep}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Soft INT4 does not create a quality separation}
\label{sec:results-int4}

Table~\ref{tab:lock240} reports the locked 240-example mixed-precision
evaluation and Figure~\ref{fig:lock240} breaks it down by context length. The
structure and role-blind arms differ by $0.0042$, equivalent to one
aggregate success on this benchmark; we do not interpret that difference as
evidence of a structure-aware quantization quality advantage. This is the
falsification of our starting hypothesis, and we report it as such.

\begin{table}[t]
  \centering
  \caption{Locked 240-example quality at matched INT4 budget
  (\texttt{int4\_frac}${=}0.75$; realized fraction $0.750$), Qwen3-8B.
  Per-stratum sizes: $n{=}83$ (8k), $81$ (16k), $76$ (32k). The
  $0.0042$ gap between the mixed arms equals one example and is not treated
  as evidence.}
  \label{tab:lock240}
  \small
  \begin{tabular}{@{}lrrrrr@{}}
    \toprule
    Arm & Mean ($n{=}240$) & $\Delta$ vs.\ \fullkv{} & 8k & 16k & 32k \\
    \midrule
    \fullkv{}                  & 0.8875 & ---      & 1.000 & 1.000 & 0.645 \\
    Role-blind mixed INT4      & 0.8792 & $-0.0083$ & 1.000 & 1.000 & 0.618 \\
    Structure-aware mixed INT4 & 0.8833 & $-0.0042$ & 1.000 & 1.000 & 0.632 \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

Two secondary observations bound the finding. At 8k and 16k, all three arms
score $1.000$, so those strata cannot separate any of the arms and the
benchmark's discriminative power for quantization lives entirely in the 32k
stratum, where \fullkv{} itself falls to $0.645$ and the mixed arms score
$0.618$ and $0.632$. And the contrast with
Section~\ref{sec:results-eviction} is the substantive conclusion: retaining
an INT4 \emph{approximation} of 75\% of positions is far less destructive
than \emph{evicting} those positions, which is why eviction and quantization
must not be pooled into a single ``compression quality'' claim.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig_quality_by_context.pdf}
  \caption{Measured lock-240 quality by context length (Qwen3-8B,
  \texttt{int4\_frac}${=}0.75$; $n{=}83/81/76$ at 8k/16k/32k; y-axis starts
  at 0.45). All three arms are saturated at $1.000$ for 8k and 16k; only the
  32k stratum separates \fullkv{} (0.645) from role-blind (0.618) and
  structure-aware (0.632) mixed INT4, and the Wilson 95\% intervals of the
  three arms overlap there, visually supporting the no-separation
  conclusion. Points are dodged horizontally for legibility. Overall means:
  0.8875, 0.8792, 0.8833 ($n{=}240$); the arm differences correspond to one
  or two examples.}
  \label{fig:lock240}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Packed storage saves bytes, costs latency}
\label{sec:results-systems}

Table~\ref{tab:latency} reports the phase-separated latency measurement and
Figure~\ref{fig:systems} summarizes the memory and latency ratios together.

\begin{table}[t]
  \centering
  \caption{Single-request latency on H200 (Qwen3-8B; 128 generated tokens;
  greedy; median of three repeats; 9 examples per stratum). \fullkv{} runs
  SDPA; the structure arm runs the packed cache with the FlashInfer shim, so
  ratios include the backend difference. Pack and cold-scratch are one-time
  per-sequence costs. The mixed path is a per-token slowdown, not a speedup.}
  \label{tab:latency}
  \small
  \begin{tabular}{@{}lrrrrrrr@{}}
    \toprule
    & \multicolumn{2}{c}{TPOT (ms)} & & & & & \\
    \cmidrule(lr){2-3}
    Context & \fullkv{} & Structure & Pack (ms) & Cold scratch (ms) &
      E2E ratio & TPOT ratio & Score \\
    \midrule
    8k  & 25.25 & 30.29 & 34.8 & 14.2 & 1.118 & 1.200 & 1.000 \\
    16k & 23.84 & 28.95 & 48.1 & 19.7 & 1.113 & 1.215 & 0.889 \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

The mixed path is close to \fullkv{} end-to-end ($1.11$--$1.12\times$)
because prefill dominates these single-request traces, but it is
approximately 20\% slower per output token; the decode-only
time-to-first-token ratio is $1.17$--$1.19\times$. Packing and cold-scratch
construction cost tens of milliseconds per sequence. We state this plainly:
the current implementation is a latency cost, not a speedup, and the
measurements exist so that the cost is not hidden behind the bytes saving.

Across the 18-example memory slice, \fullkv{} allocates 23.61\,GB at peak and
the structure-aware mixed path allocates 20.50\,GB, a ratio of $0.868$.
Actual mixed payload is 1.679\,GB against a 2.336\,GB BF16 cache model, a
ratio of $0.719$; the idealized 4-bit model gives $0.473$. The three ratios
answer different questions and must not be substituted for one another: the
modeled ratio is the ceiling a fused implementation could approach, the
payload ratio is what the packed storage actually holds (including scale
metadata), and the peak ratio is what the allocator actually experienced,
inflated by the BF16 cold scratch of Section~\ref{sec:method-decode}.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig_systems_ratios.pdf}
  \caption{Measured systems ratios for the structure-aware mixed cache,
  normalized to \fullkv{} on H200 (18-example slices; the dashed vertical
  line marks \fullkv{} parity). The three memory ratios are distinct
  quantities that must not be substituted for one another: modeled bytes
  ($0.473\times$) are the idealized 4-bit ceiling, measured payload
  including scale metadata is $0.719\times$, and peak allocated CUDA memory
  is $0.868\times$; the payload-to-peak gap is caused by the INT4-to-BF16
  cold-attention scratch. End-to-end latency ($1.113$--$1.118\times$) and
  time per output token ($1.200$--$1.215\times$) sit right of parity---a
  measured cost, reported as such.}
  \label{fig:systems}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Secondary model check}\label{sec:results-gemma}

On a reduced 14-example Gemma-2-9B-IT check at approximately 8k context with
$k{=}0.25$, \fullkv{}, structure-aware, and role-blind eviction score
$0.357$, $0.143$, and $0.000$. This supports only the direction
$\text{structure} \geq \text{role-blind}$ under the tested stress. The
absolute values are not comparable to the Qwen lock: the prompt and scorer
design and the context cap were tuned around Qwen3-8B, and \fullkv{} itself
scores only $0.357$ on this slice.

\section{Discussion}\label{sec:discussion}

The experiments support a narrow design principle: when an application
already knows the protocol role of a span, discarding that information before
cache allocation is unnecessary. Message roles are cheap, deterministic
priors that can protect tool and instruction state when attention-based
importance estimates are unavailable or stale.

The results also show why eviction and quantization should not be grouped
under a single ``compression quality'' claim. In our setup, evicting 75\% of
positions destroys the targeted agent behaviors
(Table~\ref{tab:eviction}), while storing approximately 75\% of positions in
INT4 leaves the aggregate result near \fullkv{}
(Table~\ref{tab:lock240}). A mixed-precision placement policy should
therefore be justified by memory and kernel behavior unless a harder quality
regime demonstrates otherwise.

Finally, payload accounting is not a proxy for deployed memory or
performance. The prototype stores fewer bytes but re-expands cold pages for
attention. A production implementation would need fused low-bit attention or
bounded page streaming, allocator analysis under concurrency, and integration
into a serving scheduler before claiming capacity or throughput gains.

\section{Limitations and Threats to Validity}\label{sec:limitations}

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Synthetic benchmark.} \bench{} provides controlled,
  deterministic failures but is not sampled from production agent traffic.
\item \textbf{Small positive-result slices.} The decisive eviction
  experiments use 14--16 examples, and the page-budget sweep reuses one
  fixed slice. The 240-example run evaluates mixed INT4 quality, not matched
  eviction.
\item \textbf{One primary model and device.} Qwen3-8B on H200 is the only
  complete matrix. The Gemma check is reduced, low-scoring, and directional
  only.
\item \textbf{Heuristic structure labels.} Free-form state without
  recognizable roles remains a failure mode (the buried-state score of
  $0.429$). Structural metadata can also be missing or wrong in real
  systems.
\item \textbf{Limited baselines.} Role-blind sink/recent, random,
  fixed-prefix, an FP8 check, and a reduced SnapKV attempt do not constitute
  a comparison with tuned H2O, SnapKV, PyramidKV, or state-of-the-art KV
  quantizers.
\item \textbf{Ceiling effects.} All lock-240 arms score $1.000$ at 8k and
  16k, so the quantization comparison is informative only in the 32k
  stratum.
\item \textbf{Prototype timing.} Measurements cover single-request latency,
  not batching, concurrency, throughput, or tail latency; \fullkv{} SDPA
  versus mixed FlashInfer is not a kernel-isolated comparison.
\item \textbf{Cold scratch.} INT4-to-BF16 expansion limits the realized
  peak-memory reduction and drives the measured TPOT regression.
\item \textbf{No cross-run uncertainty.} Per-example timing medians reduce
  local noise, but canonical jobs were not repeated across independent
  machine runs. Figure whiskers are Wilson 95\% intervals for within-slice
  binomial uncertainty only; they do not capture run-to-run or
  slice-to-slice variance.
\end{enumerate}

\bench{} contains generated tool contracts and synthetic identifiers rather
than personal data. The main foreseeable misuse of this report is
overgeneralizing the benchmark to claim production reliability; the frozen
claim boundary and these limitations are intended to prevent that reading.

\section{Reproducibility}\label{sec:repro}

The repository
(\url{https://github.com/Arush777/Priority_KV})
records the benchmark generator, templates, scorers, manifest, and SHA256
audit; the exact Qwen3-8B revision and non-thinking generation settings;
frozen YAML configurations and job commands; raw summaries, logs, and GPU
snapshots for the canonical jobs; CPU unit tests for policies, packed
storage, quantization, page management, and FlashInfer state logic; and
\texttt{FINAL\_RUN\_MANIFEST.yaml} as the claim and artifact index.
Table~\ref{tab:jobs} maps each headline claim to its tracked result bundle.

\begin{table}[t]
  \centering
  \caption{Claim-to-artifact index. Each job ID names a tracked bundle under
  \texttt{jobs/results/} containing the machine-readable summary, logs, and
  GPU snapshots.}
  \label{tab:jobs}
  \small
  \begin{tabular}{@{}ll@{}}
    \toprule
    Claim & Canonical job \\
    \midrule
    Latency (Table~\ref{tab:latency}) & \texttt{d4\_latency\_m3c\_gpu56\_r1} \\
    Peak and payload (Figure~\ref{fig:systems}) &
      \texttt{mg\_a\_peak\_mem\_gpu5\_r1} \\
    Lock-240 quality (Table~\ref{tab:lock240}) &
      \texttt{mg\_b\_lock240\_quality\_gpu01\_r1} \\
    Gemma reduced (Section~\ref{sec:results-gemma}) &
      \texttt{pub\_c\_gemma\_reduced\_gpu01\_r6} \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

Generate and audit the locked dataset with:
\begin{quote}
\small
\begin{verbatim}
PYTHONPATH=src uv run python scripts/mk_bench.py --mode w3_lock
PYTHONPATH=src uv run python scripts/audit_bench.py
\end{verbatim}
\end{quote}
and regenerate every paper figure from the frozen artifacts with:
\begin{quote}
\small
\begin{verbatim}
uv run python scripts/make_publication_figures.py
\end{verbatim}
\end{quote}
GPU re-execution requires the pinned model weights, the \texttt{gpu} optional
dependency set, and an H200-class CUDA environment; canonical commands and
bundle formats are documented under \texttt{jobs/} and in
\texttt{docs/REPRODUCIBILITY.md}.

\section{Conclusion}\label{sec:conclusion}

PriorityKV demonstrates that known agent-trace structure is a useful
cache-allocation signal under aggressive eviction. It does not demonstrate
that role-aware soft INT4 placement improves quality, and it does not hide
that the current packed implementation is slower per decoded token. The
complete result is a measurement result rather than a universal
cache-compression claim: preserve protocol-critical state when dropping KV,
use low precision for bytes when quality permits, and report payload, peak,
and latency as separate quantities.
